stamps.label: Football, Culture and the Conversations That Actually Happen
The Places That Stay With You

lesson.menu_desc: Telling an unforgettable story with dramatic emphasis: emphatic inversion (Never have I..., Not only..., Little did I know...) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- The Story Worth Telling -- Dramatic Emphasis
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 17 -- The Story Worth Telling

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it is Ronja. You asked me about the most jaw-dropping place I have ever seen, so here is my story. Two years ago I hiked to a glacier lagoon in the far north. Never have I seen anything so beautiful in my life. The water was a sheer, impossible blue, and huge pieces of ice floated past like slow ships. Not only was the view unforgettable, but the silence was spellbinding too. It literally stopped me in my tracks. I stood there for an hour, and honestly, the feeling was beyond words. Little did I know that a simple walk would become a once in a lifetime memory. Rarely do I call a place remarkable, but that one truly was. You have to go one day. Talk soon.

Welcome back to the show. Today, one question: why do some stories stop you in your tracks, while others we forget in a minute? Here is the secret. It is not only what happened, but how the person tells it. Great storytellers use emphasis. They do not say the view was nice. They say, never have I seen anything so beautiful. They build the moment. They pause before the surprise, and then they say, little did I know how much it would change me. Rarely do we remember a flat list of facts. But a story told with feeling, with a jaw-dropping detail and a spellbinding pause, sticks in the mind for years. So the next time you share an experience, do not just report it. Mark the moment. Make your listener feel the sheer wonder you felt. That is the story worth telling.

extra_audio.text: First, Ronja sets the scene and says she hiked to a glacier lagoon in the far north. Then she gives the big reaction: never has she seen anything so beautiful. After that, she describes the sheer blue water and the spellbinding silence. Next, she adds the twist: little did she know it would become a once in a lifetime memory. Finally, she calls the place truly remarkable and tells Marcelo he has to go.
